mnemonics.title: File Attribute Types
mnemonics.content: NIT LSP: Name, Identifier, Type, Location, Size, Protection - the 7 essential file attributes to remember!

mnemonics.title: File Access Methods
mnemonics.content: SAD: Sequential = All, Direct = Address, Indexed = Index first - simple rhyme for access types!

mnemonics.title: File Operations Order
mnemonics.content: C-O-W-R-S-D: Create → Open → Write/Read → Seek → Delete - standard sequence of file operations

mnemonics.title: File Timestamps
mnemonics.content: CMA: Creation, Modification, Access - remember 'See My Actions' to recall timestamps

mnemonics.title: Inode Contents
mnemonics.content: PMD PLT: Permissions, Metadata, Data pointers, Link count, Timestamps - what's inside an inode!

acronyms.usage: Data structure storing all file attributes and state information, loaded when file is opened
File system method using a table to track which clusters/blocks belong to each file
Marker indicating no more data to read; returned by read() when 0 bytes are read
Three essential file operations for file manipulation in any program
Two primary operations for accessing file data content

visualTips.visualization: Think of a cassette tape - to play song 5, you must fast-forward through songs 1-4. The tape head must physically move sequentially.
Think of a CD/DVD or vinyl record - you can place the needle directly on any track without playing previous ones. Record number directly maps to physical position.
Think of a book index - to find a topic, you first check the index (pointers), then go directly to that page. Two-step process.
Think of it as a ticket number at a restaurant. You get a number (descriptor) when you arrive (open), show it for service (read/write), and return it when leaving (close).
Think of a library card catalog entry - it doesn't contain the book itself (data), but has all information about the book: author, title, location, borrowing status, etc.

analogies.analogy: Like a physical file folder in an office cabinet - it's a container with a name, holding related documents (data), with metadata like creation date and owner attached to it.
Like a library card you receive when checking out a book. The library keeps track of which books are checked out using card numbers, just like OS uses file descriptors.
Like reading a book from page 1 to find a specific sentence - you must read through all previous pages. Efficient for entire file processing.
Like using page numbers in a dictionary - you can directly flip to the right page without reading all previous pages. Perfect for random lookups.
Like a delivery truck collecting multiple packages before making one trip instead of making separate trips for each package - reduces total trips (I/O operations).
